content:
5 min read
2 hours ago

--

Different tribes, different holy wars, a decade of blog posts about which one was right. They all just walked to the same spot.
I’ve been doing frontend long enough to have scar tissue. jQuery. Then Angular.js, with its $scope and its two-way binding that felt like magic until the day it really, really didn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Then React, which I learned twice, once for class components and then again when hooks landed and quietly invalidated half of what I knew. Vue at one job. A Svelte side project.
A weekend with Solid because someone on Twitter would not shut up about it.
Five frameworks, give or take, each with its own mental model I had to actually rewire my brain for, each with a comment section insisting it was the correct one and the rest were for people who didn’t get it.
Here’s the thing I’ve slowly worked out in 2026. They all became the same framework. Not officially, nobody merged. But under the hood the thing they’re all doing now is identical, and it’s the thing one of them was quietly doing the whole time while the rest of us argued.

The tell was the memoization
Let me give you the exact moment it clicked. The React Compiler is stable now, and the official guidance has flipped to, basically, stop writing useMemo and useCallback and React.memo, the compiler handles it for you at build time.
I sat there reading that and felt something between relief and grief, because I have spent real days of my life on those. The dependency arrays. The stale closure that ate an afternoon because I left one variable out of an array.
The useMemo I sprinkled onto a component out of pure superstition because someone said it was faster, with no idea whether it did a single thing. A whole craft, a whole interview topic, a whole genre of blog post, an entire cottage industry of your useMemo is wrong videos, and React just went, yeah, you should never have had to do that by hand, here’s a compiler.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The reason it landed so hard is that the entire point of doing it by hand was React’s virtual DOM. React re-runs your component, diffs a virtual tree, works out what changed. That’s why you had to memoize, to stop it doing pointless work.
The compiler doesn’t fix that model. It’s a patch over it. And the frameworks that never had a virtual DOM never had the problem to patch.

What everyone else was doing
Solid.js has been around for years doing something different and, in hindsight, just correct. Fine-grained reactivity. You make a signal, the framework knows exactly which pieces of the UI depend on that signal, and when it changes only those exact pieces update.
No re-running the component, no diffing a fake tree. No manual memoization either, because it was never doing the wasteful thing in the first place. People called it niche.
Now look at the field in 2026. Svelte 5 shipped Runes, $state and $derived and $effect, which is signals baked straight into the language. Vue always had its reactivity, and 3.6’s Vapor Mode drops the virtual DOM entirely and compiles to direct DOM operations, they demoed it mounting something like a hundred thousand components in around a hundred milliseconds.
Angular threw out its old zone.js change detection and went all in on signals in 21, and teams are reporting smaller bundles and faster loads for it. And React bolted a compiler on top to cover for the cost of the one architectural choice that made it different.
Five frameworks. Five separate communities who spent a decade certain they were built on fundamentally different ideas.
Every single one, in 2026, does fine-grained reactivity, leans on a compiler to do the optimizing, renders on the server first, and treats TypeScript as the floor. The holy wars were real. The destination turned out to be the same building.

The part that’s a little funny and a little sad
Signals aren’t even new. Knockout.js had observables in 2010. The underlying idea, that you describe what depends on what and let the machine track the updates, is older than that, it’s reactive programming with a fresh coat of paint. We had it.
We walked away from it for the virtual DOM because React made a different bet that honestly made sense at the time, a predictable component model was worth a lot, and the whole ecosystem followed the gravity of the most popular option.
Then we spent ten years discovering that bet’s hidden costs one useCallback at a time, and slowly wandered back to the idea we’d left behind.
I’m not even mad about it. That’s how this stuff moves, in big loops, not straight lines.
But I’d be lying if I said there wasn’t a small sting in watching fine-grained reactivity get crowned the future when I could have learned it properly in 2015 instead of memorizing the rules of hooks.

What this actually means for you
The practical takeaway is almost relaxing. The framework you pick matters far less than it did five years ago.
They’ve converged hard enough that the mental model transfers, learn signals in one and you basically know them in all of them, the syntax is a dialect now, not a language. The tribal you HAVE to use X energy is mostly spent, because under the paint, X and Y are the same machine.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So if you’re starting out, don’t agonize over it. Pick the one with jobs in your city, or the one whose docs you actually enjoy, and spend the energy you’d have burned on the framework war on the stuff that still genuinely varies, accessibility, the network, how data really moves through your app, the things that break at 2am. Nobody automated those.
Anyway I went back and re-read an old post of mine from 2019 defending the virtual DOM, really committing to the bit, dunking on the reactive crowd a little did not age well left it up though felt right to let past me be wrong in public, seeing as present me is just going to be wrong about something else in a year or two…
